selection_notes: Strong content throughout. The standout moments are the 38%→9% stat (segment 3), the customer email quote (segment 2), and the onboarding-as-message reframe (segment 4). Pillar coverage: Churn diagnosis (2), Onboarding clarity (2), Founder messaging (1). SaaS growth truths pillar is underrepresented — segments 8-10 may have usable content if the founder wants to add more. LinkedIn has 3 moments, Instagram 1, Twitter/X 1.

moments.quote: Most SaaS founders think their churn problem is a product problem. They're adding features, redesigning the UI, tweaking pricing. But here's the thing — churn almost always happens in week one. Before the user has ever experienced real value. So no feature you add is going to fix it.
moments.content_pillar: Churn diagnosis — how to find the real reason users leave
moments.why_social_worthy: Counterintuitive opening claim that names the exact mistake founders are making right now — stops anyone with a churn problem.
moments.positioning_angle: Directly expresses contrarian belief: features don't fix churn.
moments.hook_idea: Open with the mistake ('You think it's a product problem'). The quote is the hook.

moments.quote: The problem is that your onboarding is confusing. Users don't know what to do first. They don't understand why it matters. And they leave before the product has a chance to prove itself. I know this because I lived it. We had a 38% monthly churn rate at the exact moment we thought we had found product-market fit.
moments.content_pillar: Onboarding clarity — the gap between user expectation and first experience
moments.why_social_worthy: Personal origin story with a specific, painful stat. The '38% at the moment of PMF' detail is viscerally relatable to any founder who has been there.
moments.positioning_angle: Origin story moment — the exact experience that created the founder's expertise.
moments.hook_idea: Lead with the irony: 38% churn exactly when you thought you'd made it.

moments.quote: We hired a growth consultant. We rebuilt our pricing. We ran A/B tests on every button on the page. Nothing worked. And then a user sent us an email — a short one — that said: I still don't understand what your product actually does. That was the whole problem. Not the price. Not the features. Not the UI. The message.
moments.content_pillar: Founder messaging — how to talk about your product in your customer's language
moments.why_social_worthy: The 'one email that changed everything' story beat. The quote from the customer is the entire insight — raw and real.
moments.positioning_angle: Proves the core contrarian belief through a real customer moment.
moments.hook_idea: Build the list of things that didn't work. Land on the email. Let the customer's words be the reveal.

moments.quote: So we did something simple. We rewrote our onboarding around one job to be done. One. Not five features. Not a 12-step tutorial. One thing the user would succeed at in the first session that proved the core value of the product. Churn dropped from 38% to 9% in 60 days. We didn't ship a single new feature.
moments.content_pillar: Churn diagnosis — how to find the real reason users leave
moments.why_social_worthy: The most shareable stat in the video: 38% to 9% in 60 days with no new features. Counterintuitive proof point that validates the whole thesis.
moments.positioning_angle: The primary credibility marker from MessageDNA delivered as a story beat.
moments.hook_idea: Lead with the stat. Then work backwards to the one thing that caused it.

moments.quote: Your onboarding is a message, not a tutorial. It's not about showing users how to use your product. It's about showing users why this product is exactly what they came here to solve. That gap — between what they expect when they sign up and what they actually experience in the first five minutes — that gap is where you are losing people.
moments.content_pillar: Onboarding clarity — the gap between user expectation and first experience
moments.why_social_worthy: Clean reframe ('onboarding is a message, not a tutorial') that challenges how almost every SaaS founder thinks about the problem.
moments.positioning_angle: The core 'known for' statement in the founder's MessageDNA — stated directly.
moments.hook_idea: The reframe IS the hook: 'Your onboarding is a message, not a tutorial.'
